coredata.dc:title: A NIR luminescent “tetra-decker” Nd(III) salen nanocluster for rapid ratiometric fluorescence detection of quercetin
coredata.dc:creator: Niu, Mengyu
Leng, Xilong
Yang, Xiaoping
Schipper, Desmond
coredata.dc:description: Two NIR luminescent “bi-decker” and “tetra-decker” Cd(II)-Yb(III) and Nd(III) clusters 1 and 2 were synthesized from a new salen-type Schiff base ligand. 2 exhibits rapid ratiometric fluorescence response to Que with high sensitivity. The quenching constants of Que to the ligand-centered and lanthanide emissions of 2 are 2.9 × 104 M−1 and 3.8 × 104 M−1, respectively. The detection limits to Que are 6.75 μM for the ligand-centered emission and 2.03 μM for the lanthanide luminescence. The response of 2 to Que is not influenced by some potential interferents such as biomolecules and ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NIR luminescence
Lanthanide complexes
Schiff base ligand
Fluorescence detection
Quercetin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the Schiff base ligand H2L

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The NIR lanthanide luminescence (right) and excitation (left) spectra of 1 and 2 (35 μM) in CH3CN.

The quenching of visible and NIR emissions of 2 (35 μM) to the addition of Que in CH3CN. Inserts: (a) The linear relationship between the quenching of ligand-centered emission and Que concentrations; (b) The linear relationship between I 1067nm/I 514nm and Que concentrations. (λex = 367 nm).
The luminescence quenching of 2 (35 μM) at 1067 nm to Que and biomolecules in CH3CN. Insert: the corresponding quenching constants. (λex = 367 nm).
The luminescence quenching of 2 (35 μM) at 1067 nm before and after the addition of Que (120 μM) in the presence of the interferents (120 μM) in CH3CN.
The ligand-centered emission quenching of 2 (35 μM) at 514 nm before and after the addition of Que (120 μM) in the presence of the interferents (120 μM) in CH3CN.
The luminescence quenching of 2 (35 μM) at 1067 nm caused by the step-by-step addition of biomolecules and Que in CH3CN (10 μM each time).
Absorption spectra of Que and biomolecules in CH3CN at room temperature.
Time scan of response of 2 (35 μM) to the gradual addition of Que (15 μM each time).
The structure of salen-type ligand H2L.
The “antenna effect”, “inner filter effect” and energy transfer (ET) process in 2. (The energy levels of Schiff base ligand in 2 and Que were calculated based on their UV–vis absorption spectrum).
The photophysical data of 1 and 2. (a Lanthanide luminescence).

head.ce:title: A NIR luminescent “tetra-decker” Nd(III) salen nanocluster for rapid ratiometric fluorescence detection of quercetin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou, 325035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou, 325035, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The University of Texas at Austin, Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, 1 University Station A5300, Austin, Texas, 78712, United States

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou, 325035, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two NIR luminescent “bi-decker” and “tetra-decker” Cd(II)-Yb(III) and Nd(III) clusters 1 and 2 were synthesized from a new salen-type Schiff base ligand. 2 exhibits rapid ratiometric fluorescence response to Que with high sensitivity. The quenching constants of Que to the ligand-centered and lanthanide emissions of 2 are 2.9 × 104 M−1 and 3.8 × 104 M−1, respectively. The detection limits to Que are 6.75 μM for the ligand-centered emission and 2.03 μM for the lanthanide luminescence. The response of 2 to Que is not influenced by some potential interferents such as biomolecules and ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, fluorescent sensors have drawn much attention because of their fast response, high sensitivity and selectivity, and convenient operation [1–5]. Quercetin (3,3′,4′,5,7-penta-hydroxy-flavone, Que) is a typical flavonoid in many fruits, beverages, vegetables and herbal medicines. It is also a kind of active antioxidant with a key role in curing some cardiovascular diseases and biological cell degradation [6–8]. Some traditional analytical techniques (i.e. chromatography, capillary electrophoresis and spectrophotometry) have been employed for the detection of Que [9–11]. The fluorescence sensors used in the response of Que focus on carbon nanodots (CNDs) [12] and quantum dots (QDs) [13]. Lanthanide-based complexes can display superior visible and near-infrared (NIR) luminescence properties (e.g. long lifetimes, sharp emission peaks and large antenna-generated shifts). Currently, Bin Zhao et al. first used a Tb(III)-based metal-organic framework (Tb-MOF) as visible luminescent sensor for the detection of Que [14]. Polynuclear lanthanide clusters are attractive due to their interesting physical and chemical properties. While, so far there are no reports on the use of polynuclear lanthanide nanoclusters as fluorescent sensors to detect Que.

Salen-type Schiff bases are a class of ligands used to construct luminescent lanthanide-based complexes [15,16]. For example, “multi-decker” lanthanide salen complexes may show superior luminescence properties with the lanthanide ions shielded from the outside solvent molecules that can efficiently quench the lanthanide emission by non-radiative exchange of excitation energy [17–19]. Our research has focused on the construction of luminescent lanthanide complexes with sensing properties toward ions and small molecules [20,21]. Light-absorbing chromophores containing d-block metal ions, for instance, RuII [22], PtII [23], CrII [24], ZnII [25] and CdII [26], have been used to effectively sensitize lanthanide luminescence through ligand→f and d→f energy-transfer. In this work, we present the synthesis of two lanthanide clusters [CdYb2L2(OAc)4] (1) and [Nd5L4(NO3)4(OH)2]·OH (2) with a new salen-type Schiff base ligand N,N′-bis(3-methoxysalicylidene)(4-methoxyphenylene)-1,2-diamine (H2L, Scheme 1 ). Interestingly, 1 and 2 show “di-decker” and “tetra-decker” structures, respectively. In the nanoscale molecular structure of 2, the lanthanide ions are surrounded by the alternating layers of L2− ligands. Compared with visible fluorescence, NIR lanthanide emission has some advantages in the biological system, due to the fact that biological matrixes show low absorption from 1000 nm to 1700 nm. In the practical fluorescence detection, the self-calibration of emission signals is able to make the analysis results more accurate. Interestingly, 2 exhibits ratiometric fluorescence response to Que with the suppression of either ligand-centered or NIR Nd(III) emission.

All chemicals used in the experiments were purchased and used directly without further purified. The lanthanide compounds were synthesized using Schlenk system. IR spectra were measured on a FTIR-650 spectrometer. Elemental analyses were carried out on a EURO EA3000 with the solid sample after dried in the oven at 110 °C for 6 h. The thermogravimetric spectra were recorded on a Perking Elmer Diamond TG-DTA spectrum GX. The sample is under a N2 flow of 200 mL min−1, and heated to 900 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C per minute. Melting points were determined using a micromelting point instrument XT-4. Powder XRD spectra were obtained with a D8 Advance. The morphology of the compound was analyzed by a Nova NanoSEM 200 microscope. The sample was installed on an aluminum sheet coated with Au. The absorbance spectra of samples in solution were obtained from an UV-3600 spectrophotometer. Lanthanide luminescence spectra were performed on a FLS 980 fluorimeter. Emission quantum yields were measured using an integrating sphere, and emission lifetimes were obtained by the attached storage digital oscilloscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the Schiff base ligand H2L
ce:section.ce:para: To an ethanol solution (20 mL) of 2-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde (3.0430 g, 20.0 mmol), 4-methoxy-o-phenylenediamine (1.3817 g, 10.0 mmol) in ethanol (20 mL) was added drop by drop in 30 min with constant stirring. The mixed solution was heated under reflux for 5 h, and the red solid product was formed after cooling. The product was then filtered off and washed with ethanol (3 × 5 mL). Yield: 3.8178 g (94%). EA: Found: C, 67.92%; H, 5.41%; N, 6.87%; Calc. For C23H22N2O5: C, 67.97%; H, 5.46%; N, 6.89%. 1H NMR (δ, 500 MHz, CDCl3): 13.30 (1H), 13.10 (1H), 8.59 (2H), 7.26 (1H), 7.16 (4), 6.85 (3H), 6.71 (1H), 3.86 (9H). IR (cm−1): 2931 (w), 2834 (w), 1613 (s), 1459 (s), 1243 (s), 1194 (s), 963 (s), 804 (s), 721 (s), 635 (m).

Yb(OAc)3·4H2O (0.20 mmol, 0.0844 g), Cd(OAc)2·2H2O (0.10 mmol, 0.0266 g) and H2L (0.20 mmol, 0.0812 g) were dissolved in 20 mL of EtOH, followed by the addition of 2 mL of MeOH containing triethylamine (1.0 mol/L). After refluxing for 6 h with stirring, the mixture was filtered. The filtrate was diffused slowly by diethyl ether for one week, and yellow crystalline product was formed. The crystals were filtered off and washed with 5 mL EtOH for three times. Yield: 0.0697 g (46%). EA: C, 41.06; H, 4.02; N, 3.33% (found). Calc. for 1·2MeOH·3H2O: C, 40.83; H, 3.89; N, 3.40%. IR (KBr, cm−1): 2937.37 (m), 1617.93 (s), 1543.85 (s), 1493.68 (s), 1433.84 (s), 1382.95 (s), 1299.68 (m), 958.52 (m), 849.99 (s), 739.72 (m), 667.17 (m). m. p. > 177 °C (decompose).

The synthesis procedure of 2 was the same as that for 1, using Nd(NO3)3·6H2O (0.0877 g, 0.20 mmol) and H2L (0.0812 g, 0.20 mmol). Yield (calculated from Nd(NO3)3·6H2O): 0.0470 g (45%). EA: C, 40.21; H, 3.19; N, 5.88% (found). Calc. for 2·MeOH·5H2O: C, 40.48; H, 3.41; N, 6.09%. IR (KBr, cm−1): 3371.51 (m), 1615.20 (s), 1454.12 (s), 1382.65 (s), 1299.12 (s), 1180.32 (s), 1097.67 (m), 966.12 (m), 842.66 (s), 733.32 (m). m. p. > 176 °C (decompose).

Single crystal X-ray data were collected using a Smart APEX CCD diffractometer with graphite monochromated Mo Kα radiation. The structures of 1 and 2 were solved by direct method (WinGX program). Non-hydrogen atoms were refined with anisotropic thermal displacement parameters, and all hydrogen atoms were refined by the riding model. CCDC numbers of 1 and 2 are 2102149 and 2089106, respectively. The bond lengths and angles of the structures are listed in Tables S1 and S2.

The salen-type Schiff base ligand H2L was obtained from the reaction of 4-methoxy-o-phenylenediamine and 2-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde (yield, 94%) [27]. The 1H NMR spectrum of H2L displays the signal of imino proton (–CHN–) at 8.59 ppm (Figure S1). 1 was synthesized from the reaction of H2L with Yb(OAc)3·4H2O and Cd(OAc)2·2H2O, which shows a “di-decker” structure (Fig. 1 ). The Cd(II) ion is six-coordinated, bound to the N2O2 donor set of L2− and one OAc− anion. Two Yb(III) ions are nine-coordinated. Yb(1) is bound to the O2O2 donor set of the Schiff base ligand and two OAc− anions, while Yb(2) is coordinated with the N2O2 and O2O2 donor sets of two L2− ligands and one OAc− anion. Cd(II) and Yb(III) ions are linked together by L2− ligands and OAc− anions, and the adjacent Cd(II)···Yb(III) and Yb(III)···Yb(III) distances are 3.761 Å and 3.697 Å, respectively. The Yb–O and Yb–N bond lengths are 2.351 Å–3.041 Å and 2.506 Å–2.558 Å, respectively.

The formation of lanthanide-based complexes is metal dependent. With the same synthesis procedure, the reaction of H2L with Nd(NO3)3·6H2O gave the “tetra-decker” nanocluster 2 (Fig. 2 ). The molecular sizes of 2 are about 1.2 × 1.2 × 2.0 nm, with a C 2 rotation axis through the central Nd(1) ion. Nd(1) and Nd(2) are eight-coordinated, sandwiched between two Schiff base ligands and coordinated with the N2O2 and O2O2 donor sets. Nd(3) is nine-coordinated, located in the O2O2 cave of one Schiff base ligand. All Nd(III) ions are connected through four Schiff base ligands, and the Nd(III)···Nd(III) distances range from 3.427 Å to 3.848 Å. The Nd–O and Nd–N bond lengths are 2.205 Å–2.555 Å and 2.439 Å–2.860 Å, respectively. One scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image in Fig. 3 a displays the crystalline nature of 2, and the weight content of Nd(III) is calculated to be 27% in the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy (Fig. 3b), consistent with its crystal structure. The distribution of Nd(III) ions in 2 could be observed in the energy dispersive analysis of X-ray (EDAX) image (Fig. 3b-insert). Melting point and thermogravimetric experiments reveal that 2 is stable before 176 °C (Figure S3).

The fluorescence response properties of 2 to Que were studied in CH3CN. The results reveal that, as the concentrations of added Que increase, the ligand-centered and lanthanide emissions of 2 become weak gradually (Fig. 5 and S8), and the quenching constants (K SV , Stern-Volmer equation [29]) of Que to the emissions are calculated to be 2.9 × 104 M−1 and 3.8 × 104 M−1, respectively. Also, the limits of detection of 2 to Que for the ligand-centered and lanthanide emissions are obtained as 6.75 μM and 2.03 μM (LOD = 3σ/K sv [30]), respectively. These values are among the lowest ones reported for the fluorescence detection of Que so far [12–14,31–34]. The dual-emissive response properties of 2 to Que were further investigated through the comparison of ligand-centered and lanthanide emissions. As shown in Fig. 5 (insert), the intensity ratios of I 1067nm/I 514nm against Que concentrations are plotted, where I 1067nm and I 514nm are the emission intensities from Nd(III) ions and L2− ligands, respectively. The results demonstrate that 2 displays linear ratiometric fluorescence sensing to Que, and the corresponding fitting equation is I 1067nm/I 514nm = 0.4966–0.002*[Que].

To assess the sensing selectivity of 2 to Que in biological system, its response behavior to potential interferents, for example, some biomolecules (i.e. Fla, Hes, Nar, Phl, Dai, Res, Pue and Hyd, Scheme S1) and ions (i.e. Na+, Zn2+, K+, Ca2+, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, ClO4 − and Cl−), was investigated. Differing from Que, the addition of these interferents leads to much smaller changes to the emissions of 2 (Fig. 6 ). For instance, the quenching of ligand-centered and lanthanide emissions of 2 caused by 120 μM interferents is less than 15% (Figs. 7 and 8 and S8), suggesting the sensing selectivity of 2 to Que. The negative values of quenching percentage for some interferents (e.g. Ag+ and Cd2+) in Figs. 7 and 8
mean that their addition slightly increases the emission of 2. In addition, the sensing of 2 to Que was examined in the presence of the interferents. The results demonstrate that the quenching of ligand-centered and lanthanide emissions of 2 aroused by Que is not influenced by the interferents (Figs. 7 and 8). The fluorescence selectivity of 2 to Que was further confirmed by adding the interferents step by step. For example, the intensities of lanthanide luminescence decrease slowly with Api added twice firstly (Fig. 9 ), while, they decrease rapidly with the subsequent addition of equal amount of Que. This phenomenon occurs again with the following additions of Api and Que, indicating that 2 exhibits high selectivity to Que even in the presence of the interferent.

Compared with the interferents such as the biomolecules, Que has five phenylhydroxyl groups, which can help to form intermolecular interactions with the lanthanide nanocluster, such as hydrogen bonding between the H atoms of phenylhydroxyl groups from Que and the O atoms of NO3 − anions from 2. This may be confirmed by the UV–vis titration of Que, which shows that the absorption peaks of 2 are shifted with the addition of Que (Figure S10) [38]. For 2, based on the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the Schiff base ligand (Figure S5), its 1LC energy level is estimated to be about 29,000 cm−1, which is higher than the excited-state energy level of Que reported at 23,148 cm−1 [14]. Thus, besides the competition of excitation energy with 2, the added Que may also obtain the energy from the Schiff base ligand (Scheme 2), and further decrease the emissions of 2 [14]. The quenching of fluorescence occurs primarily through dynamic (involving the change of fluorescence lifetime) or static (no change of the lifetime) modes [39,40]. It is found that there are no obvious changes for the NIR luminescence lifetimes before and after the addition of Que (Figure S11). For example, when the concentration of Que is 120 μM, the luminescence lifetime is 6.71 μs, which is similar to that without the addition of Que, indicating that the luminescence quenching of 2 is dominated by the static model [41].

Response time is one of the most important factors for the detection of analytes. As shown in Fig. 11 , the lanthanide luminescence of 2 decreases quickly with the addition of Que, and response times are about 5 s. These results indicate that 2 shows rapid response to the addition of Que.

In conclusion, two decker-like lanthanide clusters 1 and 2 were constructed from a salen-type Schiff base ligand. The molecular sizes of the “tetra-decker” Nd(III) cluster 2 are about 1.2 × 1.2 × 2.0 nm. Both clusters show visible ligand-centered and NIR lanthanide emissions. With the addition of Que, the emissions of 2 are decreased gradually. 2 exhibits linear ratiometric fluorescence response to Que, with corresponding fitting equation: I 1067nm/I 514nm = 0.4966–0.002*[Que]. 2 displays rapid response behavior to Que with high sensitivity, which is not influenced in the presence of some interferents. The fluorescence quenching of 2 is caused by the “inner filter effect” of Que and the energy transfer between the Schiff base ligand and Que.
Xiaoping Yang conceived and designed the experiments. Mengyu Niu and Xilong Leng performed the experiments. Desmond Schipper analyzed the data.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21771141).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2022.119067.

Chem. 46 (2007) 10892-10900.
